text: The surgeon reduces the stomach into the abdominal cavity, visually repositioning it from its previous location.
The surgeon begins the initial dissection from the patient's left side, moving towards the right, using robotic instruments.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels superiorly, approaching the left crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon identifies the hernia sac and carefully dissects it free from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to bluntly and sharply dissect the left crux, then shifts attention to identifying and dissecting the right crux. The dissection continues from the left side to the right side, and the anterior vagus nerve is identified.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus and performs further posterior dissection, revealing the anatomical structures surrounding the esophagus.
The surgeon identifies the posterior vagus nerve during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the anterior vagus nerve during the dissection process.
The surgeon focuses on the hernia sac, which has been identified and is being dissected free from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs retraction and intersection, continuing dissection with excessive traction from the left side. The left posterior vagus nerve is inadvertently dissected free during this process.
The surgeon identifies the injury to the vagus nerve and prepares to perform a repair procedure.
The surgeon identifies the anterior vagus nerve and ensures its preservation during the procedure.
The surgeon cleans the edges of the vagus nerve and approximates them using floral PDAs, ensuring precise alignment of the nerve tissue.
The surgeon uses robotic assistance to place two sutures, approximating the vagus nerve. Attention is given to the hiatus during the initial approximation.
The surgeon uses CRIO V-Lock absorbable sutures followed by CRIO silk sutures in an interrupted fashion to repair the identified injury.
The surgeon reinforces the hiatal repair by placing an absorbable mesh over the repaired area.
The surgeon performs a partial fundoplication, wrapping the stomach around the lower esophagus to create a valve mechanism.
The surgeon fashions the fundoplication and secures it using serial cell sutures, completing the wrapping of the stomach around the esophagus.
The surgeon performs a pexy of the fundoplication to the diaphragm, securing the fundoplication in place using sutures.
An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to visually assess the identity and integrity of the recently completed fundoplication.
